event_blocks.summary: An ethereal, synthesized musical score opens the minute, accompanying a futuristic digital animation of a starfield and data streams that transitions into a high-speed journey through a digital tunnel, as a calm female voice declares, 'I am a visionary,' with the text 'i am a visionary' appearing. The visuals shift to a detailed astronomical image of the spiral galaxy NGC 628, labeled with scientific credits and highlighting 'Dust Enshrouded Stellar Nurseries,' while the narrator explains, 'Illuminating galaxies to witness the birth of stars.' The scene transitions to a satellite view of Taiwan, overlaid with NVIDIA CorrDiff atmospheric data showing a dramatic, swirling storm in vivid purples and oranges, as the female voice continues, 'Sharpening our understanding of extreme weather events.' The narrative progresses with a digital Earth rotating, accompanied by the text 'i am a helper,' and the narrator states, 'I am a helper.' The music becomes more uplifting as a man wearing a dotLumen head-mounted device walks on a city street with a green digital grid projected onto the pavement, while the narrator describes, 'Guiding the blind through a crowded world.' A slightly processed male voice then says, 'I was thinking about running to the store.' The video cuts to a woman in a wheelchair, connected to brain-computer interface equipment displaying her face and real-time neural data, as the narrator adds, 'And giving voice to those who cannot speak.' A distinct, processed female voice, presumably from the BCI, then articulates, 'To not make me laugh,' repeating 'laugh.' The final scene features an industrial facility with conveyor belts and machinery, overlaid with the text 'i am a transformer,' as the narrator begins to say, 'I am a...' before the minute ends with the uplifting music continuing.

event_blocks.summary: Within a large industrial facility marked by yellow railings and conveyor systems, a crane lifted a heavy object, with the 'Green Gravity xAmplify' system displaying gravitational potential energy values of 40.875 kWh and then 65.4 kWh, accompanied by a female voice stating, "I am a transformer," and "Harnessing gravity to store renewable power," as upbeat, slightly futuristic music began. The scene shifted to the U.S. Department of Energy’s PPPL fusion reactor at Princeton University, where a glowing plasma core with real-time data overlays like 'BEAM POWER' and 'INSTABILITY GROWTH DETECTED' was stabilized, followed by the voice declaring, "And paving the way towards unlimited clean energy for us all." A futuristic digital tunnel with data streams then led to a 'Fourier Intelligence' robotic trainer assisting a patient in rehabilitation under human observation, with the voice announcing, "I am a trainer," and "Teaching robots to assist." Robotic and mechanical sounds accompanied a bipedal 'Unitree' robot performing tasks in an industrial setting, operating machinery and handling tools, as the voice added, "To watch out for danger." The view transitioned to a surgical room where a 'Moon Surgical' robotic arm aided surgeons, a monitor showing a magnified view of the procedure, while the voice stated, "And help save lives." Another digital tunnel led to a molecular visualization of the 'Iambic Therapeutics NeuralPlexer', displaying a candidate molecule and binding structure prediction, as the voice proclaimed, "I am a healer," and "Providing a new generation of cures." Finally, in a modern living room, a woman using a tablet labeled 'Hippocratic AI' was shown with a dog, and the voice concluded, "And new levels of patient care," before a different female voice asked, "doctor that I am allergic to penicillin, is it still okay to take the medication?" as the music faded.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern living room, a Hippocratic AI User, seated on a couch with a dog, confirms via tablet, "Definitely. These antibiotics don't contain penicillin, so it's perfectly safe for you to take them." The scene shifts to a futuristic digital tunnel with glowing lines, where a robotic voice announces, "I am a navigator," before transitioning to a Wayve GAIA-1 prompt interface. The text 'build busy metropolitan streets' is typed, and the robotic voice states, "Generating virtual scenarios." A montage follows, depicting simulated urban driving from a vehicle's perspective, with the Wayve AI Driver navigating traffic amidst red double-decker buses and pedestrians, accompanied by car horns and traffic noise, as the voice explains, "To let us safely explore the real world and understand every decision." Next, an AI21 Labs text generation interface displays the robotic voice saying, "I even helped write the script," and "Breathe life into the words," as a rephrasing prompt generates poetic variations. This leads to a deepdub interface showing waveform editing with multilingual text overlays, such as 'and I wrote the music' and 'y escribí la música,' while the robotic voice adds, "En muchos idiomas" and "Y escribí la música." The video concludes with the London Symphony Orchestra performing, musicians playing violins and trombones under a conductor, with powerful orchestral music, crediting AIIVA for original music composition and John Paesano for arrangement. Returning to the digital tunnel, the robotic voice declares, "I am AI. Brought to life by Nvidia. Deep learning."

event_blocks.summary: The event commenced with an atmospheric stage, bathed in blue light and displaying 'i am ai' and 'MARCH 18, 2024', accompanied by ethereal music. The scene shifted to a packed, dimly lit auditorium where the NVIDIA logo was prominent on a large central screen. As the audience applauded, NVIDIA founder and CEO Jensen Wong, clad in a black leather jacket, black shirt, and white sneakers, was welcomed to the stage. He greeted the crowd with "Welcome to GTC!" and humorously clarified it was a developers conference, not a concert, promising discussions on science, algorithms, and computer architecture, which elicited laughter. Later, Jensen Huang, in similar attire and wearing a headset microphone, walked onto a dark stage under a spotlight. The auditorium, lit in purple, featured the NVIDIA logo and abstract visuals on flanking screens. Huang noted the "heavy weight" in the room, suggesting attendees might feel out of place, before emphasizing the diverse scientific fields represented, from climate tech to AI for robotics and self-driving cars, drawing applause. He quipped about a "sense of relief" and began to mention the conference's representation when the audio cut off. A black screen initially showed a dense grid of company logos, including NVIDIA, Amazon, and Microsoft, with the date and a 'y highlights' logo. Huang then appeared on stage with this logo grid behind him, clarifying that the logos represented presenters, not just attendees. He pointed out Michael Dell in the audience and highlighted that the presenters were from non-IT industries using accelerated computing for complex problems in life sciences, healthcare, transportation, retail, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial sectors, receiving further applause.

NVIDIA CEO (Jensen Huang).description: A man with gray hair, wearing a black leather jacket, black shirt, and white sneakers, equipped with a headset microphone, walking onto the stage and addressing the audience with confident gestures and a remote in hand. He speaks clearly and expressively, discussing the "amazing companies" represented by the logos on the screen, distinguishing between attendees and "presenters" from both the IT and non-IT industries, and listing various sectors like life sciences, healthcare, genomics, transportation, retail, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial that use accelerated computing.

Stage Setup.description: A large, dark stage with a central screen displaying the NVIDIA logo and flanked by two side screens with abstract visuals, set against a backdrop of illuminated company logos.

Audience.description: A large, dimly lit crowd seated in an auditorium, visible in wide shots, attentively watching the speaker on stage, and responding with enthusiastic applause and cheers at key points in the CEO's speech.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, and white sneakers, confidently walked onto a dark, spotlighted stage, addressing the audience with a headset microphone and holding a remote, as the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y highlights' logo were visible. He declared the presence of "100 trillion dollars of the world's industries" represented in the room, emphasizing the transformative impact of computing on every industry and asserting, "The industry is being transformed, not just ours." A detailed timeline graphic on a black background illustrated the evolution of computing and AI from 1964 to 2022, highlighting milestones like accelerated computing, the Transformer model, and generative AI, which Huang introduced as "NVIDIA's journey." He noted the founding in 1993, the revolutionary CUDA in 2006, the first AI and CUDA contact with AlexNet in 2012, and the invention of the DGX-1 AI supercomputer in 2016, capable of "170 teraflops," which he hand-delivered to OpenAI. Huang pointed to the timeline, highlighting the Transformer's arrival in 2017, ChatGPT's emergence in 2022, and the beginning of a new industry with generative AI in 2023, explaining, "Because the software never existed before. We are now producing software." He elaborated on this new category of software production, occurring in data centers and generating tokens at scale, drawing an analogy to the advent of electricity and factories, stating, "we are now creating new types of electrons, tokens, using infrastructure we call factories, AI factories." A technical diagram of a generative AI architecture, featuring terms like 'FINE TUNING', 'GUARDRAILING', 'ALIGNMENT', 'PROMPT ENGINEERING', 'VECTOR DB', 'RAG', 'MULTI-MODAL', 'COT & TOT', and 'AGENTS', was displayed. Huang then spoke of the "soul of NVIDIA," the intersection of computer graphics, physics, and AI within Omniverse simulations, emphasizing that "everything we're going to show you today is a simulation, not animation," and that it is "completely generated, completely simulated."

This graphic visually charts the evolution of computing and AI from 1964 IBM S/360 to 2022 OpenAI ChatGPT, featuring key milestones like accelerated computing, the Transformer model, and generative AI, alongside technical terms such as 'FINE TUNING' and 'AGENTS' linked to a generative AI architecture. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y highlights' logo are also visible. Jensen's clear, authoritative voice explains how "factories generate this new incredibly valuable thing called artificial intelligence," announcing that "a new industry has emerged." As the camera zooms in, focusing on him, he gestures expressively, declaring, "I want to show you the soul of NVIDIA. The soul of our company," highlighting the intersection of computer graphics, physics, and artificial intelligence "inside a computer in Omniverse, in a virtual world simulation." He emphasizes that "everything we're going to show you today is a simulation, not animation," attributing its beauty to physics and its animation to robotics and AI. The camera then pulls back to a wide-angle view, showcasing the full stage setup with side screens displaying abstract visuals, as Jensen walks dynamically across the stage. The central screen continues to emphasize the progression to modern generative AI and its industrial impact, while the audience remains attentively engaged as he concludes that what they are about to see is "completely generated, completely simulated."

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, and white sneakers, strode onto a dimly lit stage before a large audience, remote in hand, announcing, "what you're about to enjoy is the world's first concert where everything is homemade," and "You're about to watch some home videos. So sit back and enjoy yourself," prompting applause. A Timeline Graphic illustrating the evolution of computing and AI from 1964 to 2022 appeared on the main screen as an ethereal, wordless vocal melody began, accompanying high-quality 3D renderings of a stone cottage at dusk, a classical sculpture, a luxury watch, and a circuit board, each credited to software tools like QuadSpinner Gaea and SideFX Houdini. The scene shifted to Isaac Sim, showcasing Python code, a robot interacting with objects in a physics simulation, and a robot with a fire effect, with sounds transitioning from the vocal melody to intense mechanical grinding, whirring, and a powerful 'whoosh' or 'explosion' accompanying dynamic visuals of a complex physics demo with multiple objects and a node-based graph interface labeled Warp and PhysX. A dynamic ocean wave simulation powered by NVIDIA Warp began with a whooshing sound, followed by metallic clanking and grinding introducing a 3D rendering environment with reflective spheres and granular material labeled OpenPBR and PhysX. A shattering sound accompanied the disintegration of a 3D knight statue, demonstrating PhysX Flow’s destruction capabilities, which transitioned to a dramatic fire simulation engulfing a room with roaring fire sounds, highlighting PhysX Flow’s realistic effects. A whooshing sound accompanied a Siemens STARCCM+ with Omniverse Cloud APIs CFD simulation of airflow around a car, featuring HD Hyundai, visualized with vibrant color gradients. Mechanical whirring and clanking underscored a digital twin of an industrial facility, featuring real-time data overlays and 3D machinery models, powered by Cadence Reality Digital Twin Platform, alongside QuadSpinner Gaea and SideFX Houdini, followed by a close-up of a metallic object rendered with Warp. Robotic arm movement sounds accompanied a code editor displaying 'ov-code-gen --prompt="simulate robot gripper"', leading to a 3D simulation of a robotic arm in a factory setting labeled Isaac Sim, PowerBI, and Rockwell Automation Emulate3D, and an Amazon Robotics arm placing a pan into an oven. Complex robotic movement sounds then accompanied a simulation of Agility Robotics, Apptronik, and Unitree humanoid robots navigating an obstacle-filled environment, labeled Isaac Lab. General industrial and warehouse sounds underscored a warehouse simulation where AI-driven tools like NVIDIA NIM - DeepSearch and SimReady USD managed and organized 3D models of barrels, pallets, and forklifts, all set to a driving, futuristic instrumental background music. A SimReady USD warehouse scene demonstrated automated logistics with boxes stacked onto pallets, accompanied by whirring, clanking, and mechanical movements, transitioning to a real-world warehouse with a forklift, then a simulated aisle. The setting shifted to a shipyard where a large HD Hyundai ship was under construction, powered by Siemens Teamcenter X. A digital interface for Audio2Face NIM showed a 3D model of a human head being animated by audio input. A simulation of a city street with a yellow car navigating traffic, overlaid with sensor data from Omniverse Cloud APIs for AV Sim, followed by a similar scene using Ansys Perceive EM with a fisheye view, all while the complex soundscape continued. The segment transitioned to a global view of Earth-2, a digital twin of the planet, before concluding with a live stage presentation where a large screen displayed a 3D classical sculpture in a gallery, as the intricate mechanical and digital soundscape gradually faded out.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang confidently strode onto a dark stage, clad in his signature black leather jacket and sneakers, remote in hand, to declare that accelerated computing has reached a tipping point while general-purpose computing has run out of steam. He emphasized the need for new computing methods to scale sustainably, highlighting accelerated computing's dramatic impact across industries, particularly in simulation for product creation. The screen behind him showcased dynamic 3D simulations, initially of a white sports car with airflow visualization, branded with 'Ansys, Rescale,' and later evolving to display partnerships with Ansys, Synopsys, and Cadence. Huang announced collaborations to CUDA accelerate their ecosystems and connect them to the Omniverse Digital Twin, noting Synopsys as NVIDIA's first software partner and Cadence's role in building supercomputers with NVIDIA GPUs. He detailed the exponential growth in AI model training compute, illustrated by the 'Training Compute PFLOPs' diagram, and discussed the necessity for larger GPUs and systems, showcasing the evolution from 'SELENE 2021' to the massive 'EOS 2023' AI supercomputer. Huang elaborated on NVIDIA's comprehensive approach, building chips, systems, networking, and software, and discussed the future of AI training with multimodality data, synthetic data generation, and AI-to-AI learning, all aimed at creating larger models and increasing data utilization for grounded, common-sense AI.

raw_segments: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang walks confidently onto a dark stage, dressed in a black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, holding a remote. He smiles warmly as he approaches the center, greeted by audience applause, and exclaims, "God, I love Nvidia!" as the applause continues. He then declares that "Accelerated computing has reached the tipping point" and that "General purpose computing has run out of steam." As he speaks, the large screen behind him transitions from an abstract background to a high-resolution 3D simulation of a white sports car in a modern industrial environment, featuring dynamic, color-coded airflow visualization (orange, yellow, blue) over its body, with 'Ansys, Rescale' displayed in the bottom left. Jensen Huang gestures toward this visual, explaining, "We need another way of doing computing so that we can continue to scale, so that we can continue to drive down the cost of computing, so that we can continue to consume more and more computing while being sustainable." He emphasizes that "Accelerated computing is a dramatic speedup over general purpose computing," and that its "impact is dramatic" in "every single industry." He asserts, "But in no industry is it more important than our own," referring to "the industry of using simulation tools to create products." The camera alternates between wide shots of him against the massive, vivid screen and closer shots of his expressive hand movements, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y! highlights' logo consistently visible.

The minute opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, confidently walking onto a dimly lit stage under a spotlight, remote in hand. He immediately begins his presentation, gesturing expressively as he declares the industry's focus is on "driving up the scale of computing," not down the cost. The background screen, initially displaying abstract blue and white motion graphics, transitions to a large, detailed 3D simulation of a white sports car in an industrial setting, overlaid with vibrant orange, yellow, and blue airflow visualizations, visually demonstrating aerodynamic performance. The text 'Ansys, Rescale' appears in the bottom left corner, identifying the simulation software. Huang emphasizes the goal to "simulate the entire product... completely in full fidelity, completely digitally" through "digital twins," and to "design it, build it, simulate it, operate it completely digitally." He moves across the stage, engaging the audience, occasionally pointing toward the screen as he states the necessity to "accelerate an entire industry" and announces partners joining this journey to "accelerate their entire ecosystem" into "accelerated computing." He then reveals a "bonus": accelerated infrastructure becomes "CUDA GPUs," which is "exactly the same infrastructure for generative AI." The camera alternates between close-ups of him and wide shots of the large, attentive audience in the darkened auditorium. The stage setup includes a central screen flanked by side visuals, with directional signage (A2, A3) visible. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y! highlights' logo are consistently displayed, reinforcing the professional, high-tech atmosphere centered on innovation in simulation and AI-driven design.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, walks onto a stage holding a remote. The stage, initially lit with a dynamic blue background, transitions to a dark, starry black backdrop as he begins his presentation. Speaking with an enthusiastic and clear voice, he expresses his delight to announce "several very important partnerships" with "some of the most important companies in the world." As he mentions "Ansys," the screen behind him displays the Ansys and NVIDIA logos, and he elaborates on the partnership to "CUDA accelerate the Ansys ecosystem" and "connect Ansys to the Omniverse Digital Twin," calling it "incredible." He continues, emphasizing the global reach of NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems. The screen then changes to a detailed slide titled 'SYNOPSYS: MISSION CRITICAL FOR NVIDIA SILICON SUCCESS,' showcasing performance improvements across various EDA tools like Verification, Design, Simulation, Generative AI, and Systems Software, with specific NVIDIA hardware like Grace Hopper and cuLitho highlighted by performance multipliers (e.g., 13X, 10X, 15X). Huang then announces "Synopsys" as "NVIDIA's literally first software partner," stating they were there from "the very first day of our company" and "revolutionized the chip industry with high-level design," confirming they are "going to CUDA accelerate." Throughout, Huang gestures expressively, and the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is consistently displayed in the top right corner, with a 'y! highlights' watermark in the bottom right, while the lighting remains focused on him, keeping the audience in darkness.

The video opens with a close-up of NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, standing under a spotlight on a dark stage. He presents dynamically, using expressive hand gestures while holding a remote and speaking into a headset microphone. The background initially displays 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y! highlights' logo. Jensen Huang begins by praising "great Synopsys" and explaining, "We're accelerating computational lithography, one of the most important applications that nobody's ever known about," emphasizing the need to "push lithography to the limit" to make chips. He reveals that "NVIDIA has created a library, a domain-specific library, that accelerates computational lithography incredibly." As he speaks, the camera pulls back to reveal a wide stage with a large screen behind him, which first shows the logos of Synopsys and NVIDIA, then transitions to a detailed technical slide titled 'cadence + NVIDIA: Deep collaboration on EDA, SDA, Digital Biology and AI,' showcasing NVIDIA HGX H100 and Cadence Palladium & Protium, highlighting accelerated compute and principled simulation. Huang continues, noting that "once we can accelerate and software define all of TSMC, who is announcing today that they're going to go into production with NVIDIA Cuda Litho," the next step is to "apply generative AI to the future of semiconductor manufacturing, pushing geometry even further." The screen then displays a technical diagram illustrating AI concepts like 'FINE TUNING' and 'AGENTS' within a generative AI architecture. He then introduces, "Cadence builds the world's essential EDA and SDA tools," adding, "We also use Cadence. Between these three companies, Ansys, Synopsys, and Cadence, we basically build NVIDIA." The camera alternates between close-ups of Huang and wide shots of the attentive, dimly lit audience, as he concludes by stating, "Together, we are accelerating Cadence. They're also building a supercomputer out of NVIDIA GPUs." The stage lighting remains focused on Huang, creating a dramatic contrast with the dark surroundings, while the evolving screen content underscores strategic partnerships and technological advancements.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, stands alone under a single spotlight on a dark stage, holding a remote and gesturing expressively. He addresses a large, dimly lit audience, speaking about enabling customers to perform "fluid dynamic simulation at a hundred, a thousand times scale," effectively creating "a win tunnel in real-time." He highlights "Cadence Millennium, a supercomputer with NVIDIA GPUs inside," expressing his admiration for "a software company building supercomputers." As he speaks, the stage backdrop transitions to display the Cadence and NVIDIA logos side by side, emphasizing their collaboration. Huang continues, detailing plans for "building Cadence co-pilots together" and envisioning a future where tool providers like "Cadence, Synopsys, Ansys" offer "AI co-pilots" to create "thousands and thousands of co-pilot assistants helping us design chips, design systems." He announces the connection of "Cadence Digital Twin Platform to Omniverse." Concurrently, a technical slide appears on the screen, titled 'Training Compute PFLOPs,' illustrating the exponential growth in computational demands for AI models like AlexNet, GPT-3, and GPT-MoE-1.8T from 2012 to 2024. Huang concludes by stating they are "accelerating the world's CAE, EDA, and SDA so that we could create our future in digital twins," and will "connect them all to Omniverse, the fundamental operating system for future digital twins."

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, walks across a dimly lit stage, holding a remote. He stands before a large screen displaying a technical diagram titled 'Training Compute PFLOPs,' which visually plots the exponential growth of AI model training compute from 2012 to 2024 on a logarithmic scale, featuring distinct blue and steeper green trend lines for various AI models. Speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, Huang explains that large language models have "benefited tremendously from scale," particularly after the Transformer was invented, allowing them to "scale... at incredible rates," effectively "doubling every six months." As he gestures towards the graph, the camera zooms in, highlighting his emphasis on the rapid increase in computational demands, especially after 2018, an inflection point where the green line diverges sharply. He elaborates that this doubling of model size and "parameter count" necessitates a corresponding increase in "training token count," leading to the massive "computation scale" required. Huang points out that the "state of the art Open AI model is approximately 1.8 trillion parameters," directly relating to the advanced models shown on the steep green curve of the diagram, underscoring NVIDIA’s role in enabling this AI compute revolution. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y highlights' logo are visible, setting the context of this high-tech presentation.

The segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, commanding a dark stage under focused lighting. He presents dynamically, using expressive hand gestures while holding a remote and speaking into a headset microphone. The background initially displays large white numbers like '2016' and '2018', suggesting a timeline, before transitioning to a detailed technical slide titled 'Training Compute PFLOPs'. This diagram plots the exponential growth of AI model training compute from 2012 to 2024, marking models like AlexNet, GPT-3, and GPT-MoE-1.8T on a logarithmic scale. As Jensen Huang gestures towards this graph, he explains the immense computational requirements, stating, "1.8 trillion parameters required several trillion tokens to go train." He elaborates on "a few trillion parameters on the order of, a few trillion tokens on the order of," leading to "approximately 30, 40, 50 billion quadrillion floating-point operations per second." He then humorously asks the audience to "just hang with me" for some "CEO math," explaining that a "quadrillion is like a peta." He continues, "if you had a petaflop GPU, you would need 30 billion seconds to go compute, to go train that model. 30 billion seconds is approximately 1,000 years." A brief, appreciative laugh from the dimly lit audience, visible in wider shots, follows his statement, "Well, 1,000 years, it's worth it." He then adds, "I'd like to do it sooner, but it's worth it," eliciting more audience reaction. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y! highlights' logo are visible on screen, underscoring the professional and dynamic atmosphere of NVIDIA's AI innovation presentation.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dimly lit stage, holding a remote and speaking into a headset microphone. He begins with a humorous remark about how his answer to 'how long' is often '20 years,' adding 'it's worth it,' which elicits laughter from the attentive, dimly lit audience. He then playfully asks, 'But can we do it next week?' before emphatically stating, '1000 years, 1000 years.' Behind him, a large screen initially displays a 'Training Compute PFLOPs' technical diagram, illustrating the exponential growth of AI model training compute from 2012 to 2024, with key models like AlexNet, GPT-3, and GPT-MoE-1.8T marked. Huang gestures expressively as he walks, emphasizing the rapid advancements and declaring, 'what we need are bigger GPUs,' and 'much, much bigger GPUs.' He explains that NVIDIA recognized this early on, leading them to 'put a whole bunch of GPUs together,' inventing 'Tensor Cores' and advancing 'NVLink' to create 'virtually giant GPUs.' He highlights connecting these with 'amazing networks' from 'Mellanox InfiniBand' to build 'these giant systems.' The screen then transitions to a split-screen comparison of AI supercomputers: 'SELENE 2021' (4,480 A100 GPUs) and 'EOS 2023' (10,752 H100 GPUs), showcasing a dramatic increase in AI compute and interconnect bandwidth. Huang moves between the two sides, pointing to each system as he mentions DGX-1 was their 'first version' and how they've continued to 'build supercomputers all the way.' The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y highlights' logo remain visible, framing the presentation of these significant technological leaps.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark stage under a spotlight, presenting in front of a large screen. He speaks with a clear, authoritative, and passionate voice, detailing the evolution of AI supercomputing. The screen behind him displays a technical comparison, with 'SELENE 2021' on the left, showing 4,480 A100 GPUs, 3 EF AI Compute, and 112 TB/s Interconnect BW. Huang refers to this, stating, "In 2021, we had Selene, 4500 GPUs or so." He then transitions to the right side of the screen, which features a more complex, colorful 3D model of server racks labeled 'EOS 2023' with 10,752 H100 GPUs, 43 EF AI Compute, and 1,100 TB/s Interconnect BW, proclaiming, "And then in 2023, we built one of the largest AI supercomputers in the world. It's just come online. EOS." He gestures expressively with his hands, holding a remote, emphasizing the scale and performance leap. The camera zooms in on him as he speaks passionately, using broad hand movements to convey the magnitude of these advancements. He explains NVIDIA's comprehensive approach: "We build the chips, the systems, the networking, all of the software necessary to do this." He further elaborates on the complexity and efficiency, stating, "Imagine writing a piece of software that runs across the entire system, distributing the computation across thousands of GPUs, but inside are thousands of smaller GPUs. Millions of GPUs to distribute work across all of that and to balance the workload so that you can get the most energy efficiency, the best computation time, keep your cost down." The scene transitions to a wider shot, showing him alone on the stage with the audience visible in the dimly lit background, reinforcing the scale of the event, as he concludes by mentioning "those fundamental innovations."

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, illuminated by a spotlight on a dark stage, wearing his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, dark pants, and white sneakers, begins his presentation with expressive hand gestures, holding a remote. He passionately states, "is what God is here. And here we are, as we see the miracle of Chat GPT emerge in front of us, we also realize we have a long ways to go." As he speaks, a large central screen displays a technical diagram comparing 'SELENE 2021' (4,480 A100 GPUs, 3 EF AI Compute, 112 TB/s Interconnect BW, white server racks) with 'EOS 2023' (10,752 H100 GPUs, 43 EF AI Compute, 1,100 TB/s Interconnect BW, colorful server racks), with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y! highlights' logo visible. Huang walks across the stage, gesturing toward the screen, emphasizing the need for "even larger models" and training with "multimodality data, not just text on the internet, but we're going to train it on texts and images and graphs and charts." He continues, explaining how models will learn by "watching TV" and "a whole bunch of watching video" to be "grounded in physics" and develop "common sense," understanding "an arm doesn't go through a wall." The dimly lit audience attentively watches as Huang underscores his points with hand gestures, discussing the use of "synthetic data generation" and imagination in learning, much like his own preparation for the keynote.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black leather jacket and shirt, addressed a large audience on a dimly lit stage, announcing the development of larger GPUs beyond Hopper, culminating in the introduction of the Blackwell platform, named in honor of mathematician David Blackwell. The announcement was accompanied by loud applause and cheers, as a massive screen displayed a technical diagram comparing AI supercomputing systems. A distinct whoosh and deep thrumming sound marked the transition to a close-up of the 'BLACKWELL' chip, noted as 'THE LARGEST CHIP PHYSICALLY POSSIBLE' with 104 billion transistors, followed by a 'BLACKWELL GPU' slide detailing 'THE TWO LARGEST DIES POSSIBLE—UNIFIED AS ONE GPU' with 208 billion transistors. A 'BLACKWELL VERSUS HOPPER' slide highlighted a 'MASSIVE LEAP IN COMPUTE' with twice the size and 5x AI performance. A persistent, high-pitched electronic whirring and buzzing soundscape accompanied visuals of the BLACKWELL chip, described as 'THE ENGINE OF THE NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION,' boasting 20 petaFLOPS of AI performance and 192GB of HBM3e. The integration of two BLACKWELL GPUs and one GRACE CPU formed the GB200 superchip, featuring 384GB of HBM3e and 72 Arm Neoverse V2 CPU cores, with the sounds intensifying to resemble data processing. The GB200 GRACE BLACKWELL SUPERCHIP was presented as a unified processor for trillion-parameter-scale generative AI, offering 40 petaFLOPS and 864GB of fast memory. The BLACKWELL COMPUTE NODE, featuring two Grace CPUs and four Blackwell GPUs, delivered 80 petaFLOPS and utilized a liquid-cooled MGX design. The CONNECTX-800G INFINIBAND SUPERNIC and BLUEFIELD-3 DPU were also showcased, optimized for AI processing and infrastructure tasks respectively. The GB200 NVL72 was presented as a modern data center compute solution, comprising 18 compute trays, 36 Grace CPUs, and 72 Blackwell GPUs, accompanied by precise mechanical clicks and whirs. A distinct 'thump' introduced the NVLink Switch with its 1.8TB/s ports, aggregating to 14.4TB/s, connected by light blue cables with subtle electronic zaps. The NVLink Switch System, featuring 18 NVLink Switch chips and 72 ports, enabled all-to-all communications at 130TB/s, integrating GB200 Compute Nodes, NVLink Switch, and Spine into 'one massive GPU for generative AI,' fully connected by NVLink using cost-effective copper cabling and blind-mate connectors. The GB200 NVL72 was further detailed to deliver 1.4 exaFLOPS of AI performance and 30TB of HBM3e, functioning as a giant CUDA GPU, punctuated by a final powerful 'thump' and electronic whine. Amidst continuous mechanical sounds, the Quantum InfiniBand Switch offered 800Gb/s per port and 230.4Tb/s aggregated throughput, alongside multiple Quantum-X800 InfiniBand or Spectrum-X800 Ethernet switches providing 14.4 teraFLOPS of in-network computing. The segment concluded with a full-scale deployment of eight GB200 NVL72 compute racks, totaling 288 Grace CPUs and 576 Blackwell GPUs, featuring 2X reduced rack cooling power and a liquid-cooling system, scaling into a vast data center environment, all underscored by a deep, pulsing hum signifying immense, interconnected compute power for generative AI, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y highlights' logo consistently visible.

event_blocks.summary: On March 18, 2024, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black leather jacket, commanded a dark, minimalist stage under focused lighting, presenting the NVIDIA Blackwell platform to a dimly lit audience. He began by holding up two small, square black chips, identifying one as 'Hopper' and the other, labeled 'NVIDIA GB200' and 'GH100-BB0C-A1,' as the 'heart of the Blackwell system,' which he clarified is a platform, not just a chip, containing 208 billion transistors with two abutted dies capable of 10 terabytes per second of data transfer, making them unaware of their separation. Huang then showcased the GB200 NVL72 compute node, a large, modular system designed to be form-fit-function compatible with existing Hopper infrastructure, emphasizing the ease of upgrading. He detailed the platform's advancements, including a second-generation transformer engine, 5th generation NVLink with computation in the network offering 1.8 terabytes per second, a RAS engine for 100% self-testing of every component, and Secure AI for encrypting data at rest, in transit, and during computation. Huang highlighted new numerical formats like FP6 and FP4, which amplify bandwidth and parameter storage, with FP4 effectively doubling throughput for inference and content token generation, offering 5X the token generation and inference capability of Hopper. He contrasted this with past retrieval-based computing, asserting that 'the future is generative' and that NVIDIA created a processor specifically for this new era, noting a 1,000X increase in AI compute over eight years, far surpassing Moore's Law. Throughout the presentation, technical diagrams of the Blackwell platform, AI Superchip, and GPU performance metrics were displayed on a large screen, accompanied by the 'y/ highlights' logo and the date.

raw_segments: The video opens with a sweeping 3D animation of a massive data center, filled with NVIDIA-labeled server racks, showcasing its scale with 32,000 GPUs, 645 exaFLOPS of AI performance, and other impressive specs, positioning it as an 'AI Factory for the New Industrial Revolution,' accompanied by a distinct whooshing sound. The scene transitions to NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, confidently walking across a dimly lit stage with focused lighting, while the audience applauds and cheers. He gestures towards a large screen displaying a technical diagram of the NVIDIA Blackwell platform. Jensen then picks up two small, square, black chips, holding them up to the audience. The camera zooms in on one, revealing 'NVIDIA GB200' and 'GH100-BB0C-A1'. Jensen explains, "Blackwell is not a chip. Blackwell is the name of a platform," and that "GPUs don't look the way they used to." He identifies the chip as "the heart of the Blackwell system," noting that internally, it's "just a number." He then refers to the other chip as "the most advanced GPU in the world in production today," which he explicitly names as "Hopper," prompting further enthusiastic applause from the attentive audience. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y highlights' logo are consistently displayed.

On March 18, 2024, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, illuminated by focused lighting on a dark stage, wearing a black leather jacket over a black shirt, presents two small, square, black chips. He holds one in each hand, identifying the first as "Hopper," stating it "changed the world," then introduces the second as "Blackwell," which is met with immediate audience applause and cheers. A close-up reveals the chip in his right hand, labeled 'NVIDIA GB200' and 'GH100-BB0C-A1'. Playfully, he reassures "Hopper" that "it's okay," calling it "very good" and a "good boy," then "good girl." Bringing both chips closer to the camera to emphasize their size and design, he gestures expressively as he details the new chip, explaining it contains "208 billion transistors." He highlights a "small line between two dies," noting it's the first time two dies have been abutted in such a way that they "think it's one chip," facilitating "10 terabytes of data between it, 10 terabytes per second," making the two sides of the Blackwell chip unaware of their separation. The scene is framed by a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right and the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' in the top right, with the black background focusing all attention on Huang and his groundbreaking hardware.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark, focused-lit stage, gesturing towards a large screen displaying the 'NVIDIA BLACKWELL PLATFORM' announcement from March 18, 2024. The screen features a detailed diagram of the BLACKWELL chip architecture, highlighting its 208 billion transistors and advanced engines. Jensen explains, "There's no memory locality issues, no cache issues. It's just one giant chip," recounting how engineers overcame perceived "limits of physics" to create it. He then moves to a black display table, picking up and presenting the large, modular GB200 NVL72 compute node, showcasing its stacked design with multiple NVIDIA-branded GPU modules from various angles. He elaborates that this system, housing the Blackwell chip, is designed for "two types of systems," with the first being "form-fit-function compatible to Hopper." He emphasizes the efficiency of upgrading existing Hopper installations by simply sliding out Hopper and pushing in Blackwell, leveraging the same infrastructure, power, thermals, and software, calling it the "Hopper version for the current HGX configuration." The 'y highlights' logo is visible throughout the segment.

On a dark stage illuminated by focused lighting, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket over a black shirt, begins by holding a small, rectangular circuit board, which he identifies as a "prototype board." He walks across the stage, then asks, "Janine, could I just borrow," before a woman in black attire, whom Huang introduces as "Ladies and gentlemen, Janine Paul," enters from stage left to hand him a larger, more complex circuit board. The audience responds with applause and cheers for Janine. Huang then holds both boards side-by-side, showcasing the size difference and technological evolution. He identifies the larger board, the GB200 NVL72 compute node, as a "fully functioning board" and a "bring up board," humorously suggesting it's worth "10 billion dollars," which elicits laughter from the audience. He then quips that the smaller board is "five" billion, drawing more laughter, and assures "any customers in the audience, it's okay" as "it gets cheaper after that." The larger board features a prominent central chip and two large square components resembling eyes. Huang gestures toward it, emphasizing its features, while the camera zooms in to reveal intricate circuitry, including the NVIDIA logo and multiple connectors. He explains that the production version will be "like this one here," referring to the smaller board, which he clarifies has "two Blackwell die." The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right corner, and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo appears in the bottom right, all contributing to an atmosphere of high-tech revelation as Jensen Huang presents the hardware as a breakthrough in AI computing.

event_blocks.summary: On a dark, minimalist stage, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black leather jacket and headset mic, presented the DGX GB200 NVL72 system, highlighting its unprecedented scale and performance. He initially showcased the NVLink Switch Chip, detailing its '50B Transistors in TSMC 4NP' and '7.2TB/sec Full-Duplex Bandwidth', emphasizing 'direct drive to copper' for system construction. The DGX GB200 NVL72 was then revealed as '1 Giant GPU' capable of '720 PFLOPS' for Training FP8 and '1.44 ExaFLOPS' for Inference FP4, a significant leap from the first DGX-1 delivered to OpenAI six years prior, which had 0.17 petaflops. Huang detailed the system's '720 PFLOPS' training and '1.44 ExaFLOPS' inference capabilities, noting it as the 'world's first one ExaFLOPS machine in one rack' and one of only a few such machines globally. He underscored the system's internal bandwidth, stating its 'DGX MVLink spine' achieved '130 terabytes per second' through its chassis, exceeding the aggregate bandwidth of the internet, facilitated by '5,000 NVLink cables' spanning '2 miles'. Huang also explained the efficiency of the NVLink switch, saving '20 kilowatts for computation' and noting the entire rack consumes '120 kilowatts', with a liquid cooling system circulating water at '2 liters per second' from 25 degrees C to 45 degrees C. He contrasted the current server modules, representing '600,000 parts', with older 'HGX' GPUs that were '70 pounds' and had '35,000 parts', noting the modern GPUs weigh '3,000 pounds'. Huang further illustrated the scale by stating that training a 'GPT model, 1.8 trillion parameter model' with '8,000 GPUs' would require '15 megawatts' and take '90 days'. The presentation, occurring on March 18, 2024, featured on-screen text like 'Train GPT-MoE-1.8T in 90 Days' and 'Hopper 8000 GPUs | 15MW', and was accompanied by audience applause and laughter, with a 'y/ highlights' logo visible throughout.

Jensen Huang further details its 'DGX MVLink spine' achieving '130 terabytes per second' through its chassis, connected by '5,000 NVLink cables' spanning '2 miles', and highlights its power efficiency by contrasting it with the '20,000 watts' or '2 kilowatts' that would be required for optics transceivers alone.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black leather jacket and glasses, stood on a dark, minimalist stage under focused lighting, holding a remote and gesturing expressively as he addressed a large, dimly lit audience. On-screen text displayed 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo. Huang initially discussed the immense computational cost of training groundbreaking AI models, citing a previous requirement of 8,000 GPUs and 15 megawatts of power. He then presented a technical diagram of a Blackwell GB200 NVL72 server rack, illustrating that with Blackwell, training the same model would only require 2,000 GPUs and 4 megawatts over 90 days, a significant power reduction. He emphasized NVIDIA's commitment to reducing computing costs and energy consumption to scale next-generation models, differentiating between training and inference. Huang further elaborated on inference, explaining that it powers applications like co-pilot and ChatGPT, involves trillions of tokens and parameters, and is challenging because models often exceed single GPU capacity, using analogies of Excel or video games not fitting on one device. He highlighted the need for supercomputers to run single inference applications and the importance of generating tokens at interactive rates for chatbots. Huang then presented a performance graph titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference,' plotting 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second,' with the 'upper right' representing optimal performance. He showcased the superior 'GB200 FP4' green line, explaining its performance was achieved through 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization' strategies like tensor, expert, and data parallelism, distributing the enormous model across many GPUs. Huang credited the programmability of NVIDIA's GPUs and the CUDA ecosystem for enabling this exploration and achieving the 'green roofline.' He detailed specific parallelization configurations, such as 'TP2.EP8.DP4,' and noted how different runtime configurations yield varied results. Huang concluded by stating that Blackwell's inference capability is 'off the charts,' approximately 30 times that of Hopper, a leap achieved not by changing Hopper's architecture but by creating a bigger chip and enhancing connectivity, as visually supported by the performance graph comparing GB200 FP4 against B200 FP8 and H200 FP8.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black textured leather jacket and white sneakers, stood center stage under a spotlight, gesturing expressively as he announced "Unbelievable excitement for Blackwell!" event_blocks.summary: On March 18, 2024, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stood on a dark, minimalist stage holding a small black remote. Huang announced the integration of the NVIDIA ecosystem, including DGX Cloud, Omniverse, and Healthcare, into Azure, emphasizing its deep connection with Microsoft Fabric. He then focused on the 'Blackwell' architecture, stating the industry's anticipation and presenting 'full fidelity designs' of Blackwell, explaining the 'digital twin' concept for building complex entities perfectly on the first attempt.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, pants, and white sneakers, commanded the dark stage, holding a small black remote and gesturing expressively. He introduced the concept of creating digital twins for manufacturing, stating, "Creating a digital twin to build a computer that was built in a digital twin." The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo were displayed as upbeat corporate music began. The scene shifted to NVIDIA server racks and the Wistron logo, with a narrator explaining Wistron's use of Omniverse SDKs and APIs to build digital twins of NVIDIA DGX and HGX factories for accelerated computing. A detailed 'Omniverse Digital Twin' of a factory floor, complete with machinery, conveyor belts, and real-time status indicators, was showcased. Wistron initially used a digital twin to integrate multi-CAD and simulation data, a process illustrated by a technical diagram showing integration with NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via USD and Omniverse Channel APIs. This digital environment increased worker efficiency by 51%. The video then demonstrated augmented reality applications, overlaying the digital twin onto a real, unfinished industrial space, allowing verification of the physical build against digital plans and avoiding costly change orders. A person used a tablet, and a woman wearing an Apple Vision Pro headset interacted with the virtual overlay in the physical space. The segment transitioned to a 3D simulation of the Omniverse Digital Twin, highlighting automated industrial facilities with status lights and UPH metrics, and an NVIDIA component within a server rack. The narrator revealed that the Omniverse Digital Twin helped bring Wistron's factory online in two and a half months, enabling rapid layout testing, operational improvements, and real-time monitoring via IoT data, reducing end-to-end cycle times by 50% and defect rates by 40%. Huang then took the stage, declaring, "That's how we... That's the way it's going to be in the future, we're manufacturing everything digitally first, and then we'll manufacture it physically." He then delved into the origins of AI, referencing the 2012 AlexNet 'First Contact' with AI vision, showing a kitten photo leading to a neural network schematic and a 'CAT' label, explaining how AI could reduce a million dimensions to three letters, a single vector, and generalize to recognize different cats. Huang emphasized this as a "completely new way of writing software," where typing 'C-A-T' results in a cat. He further elaborated on AI's ability to understand meaning beyond pixels, referencing a complex diagram titled 'LEARN AND UNDERSTAND EVERYTHING' with various inputs and outputs, and its capacity to condition between modalities, like language conditioning an image. Huang then shifted to the potential of digitizing and learning from structures like proteins, genes, and brainwaves, leading to the generative AI revolution. He introduced the concept of 'Earth 2,' a digital twin of Earth for weather prediction, and 'CorrDiff,' a generative AI model for high-resolution weather forecasting. Dramatic music accompanied a split-screen montage of storm footage and WION news tickers, illustrating the impact of Super Typhoon Chanthu. The limitations of typical 25 km resolution AI forecast models were contrasted with the dramatically more detailed 'CorrDiff 2 km resolution' view. The speaker explained that CorrDiff uses high-resolution radar-assimilated data to super-resolve extreme events, achieving significantly higher speed and energy efficiency than conventional models. Combining NVIDIA's FourCastNet and CorrDiff allows for predicting storm impacts, minimizing loss of life and property, as shown by an ensemble of typhoon track predictions. CorrDiff, initially optimized for Taiwan, will soon be globally available as part of the NVIDIA Earth-2 inference service.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small remote and gesturing animatedly. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, commanded a dark, minimalist stage, wielding a small black remote to punctuate his dynamic presentation. He initiated the discourse by highlighting The Weather Company, identified as the 'trusted source of global weather predictions,' and detailed NVIDIA's collaboration to 'accelerate their weather simulation' and implement 'Earth-2 Co-Dev' for advanced 'regional, high-resolution weather prediction.' Huang then pivoted to 'NVIDIA HEALTHCARE,' a division established '15 years ago' and a source of immense pride, showcasing its four pillars: IMAGING & ROBOTICS (MONAI | Holoscan), GENOMICS (Parabricks), BIOINFORMATICS (RAPIDS and pandas), and DRUG DISCOVERY (BioNeMo), each accompanied by striking 3D visualizations. He emphasized NVIDIA's foundational role in 'medical imaging,' 'gene sequencing,' and 'computational chemistry,' stating NVIDIA provides 'the computation behind it.' Huang revealed plans for a 'really, really cool' initiative integrating 'all of these AI models.' Transitioning to the digitization of biological data, he explained how NVIDIA's work extends to the 'digitization that we've done for genes and proteins and amino acids,' now processed through machine learning to understand 'the language of life.' He drew a powerful comparison between traditional protein reconstruction methods, which yielded approximately 200,000 proteins over decades, and AlphaFold's achievement of reconstructing '200 million proteins' in 'less than a year,' effectively mapping 'every protein.' The on-screen display featured 'MARCH 18, 2024' in the top right and the 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right, underscoring the genomic and drug discovery themes central to Huang's compelling narrative.

The content of the slide is directly referenced and elaborated upon by Jensen Huang's speech, particularly the "GENOMICS" and "DRUG DISCOVERY" sections, as he discusses protein reconstruction and AlphaFold.

event_blocks.summary: On a dark, minimalist stage, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black leather jacket, announced revolutionary models for every sequenced living thing, acknowledging their complexity and NVIDIA's commitment to researchers worldwide, as 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo appeared on screen. The presentation shifted to 'NVIDIA HEALTHCARE' domains—IMAGING & ROBOTICS, GENOMICS, BIOINFORMATICS, and DRUG DISCOVERY—illustrated with 3D visualizations and tools like MONAI, Parabricks, RAPIDS, pandas, and BioNeMo. A female narrator introduced NVIDIA BioNeMo NIMS as a solution to the computationally intractable virtual screening of new medicines, which previously took days to scan billions of compounds. This was visualized by a technical flowchart detailing a drug discovery pipeline integrating AlphaFold2 for protein structure prediction, MolMIM for molecule generation, and DiffDock for docking, with a 'Gradient-Free Optimization' step, enabling candidate generation and screening in minutes. The narrator further explained that custom applications could steer this generative process using BioNeMo microservices or custom builds, demonstrated by a 3D visualization of evolving molecular structures analyzed via 2D and 3D scatter plots (LogP, Molecular Weight, QED) during a physics-based simulation optimizing for binding affinity and favorable molecular properties. Histograms comparing 'reference' and 'generated' distributions for Docking Score, QED, and LogP, followed by four molecular docking simulations, underscored MolMIM's ability to generate high-quality, drug-like, and synthesizable molecules. The NVIDIA BioNeMo web interface, a cloud service for generative AI in drug discovery, was then showcased with a protein folding simulation. The speaker elaborated on BioNeMo NIMS providing on-demand microservices for workflows like de novo protein design and guided molecule generation. A Jupyter notebook interface displayed Python code for molecular visualization and 3D protein-molecule interaction, followed by a 'Playground' interface where a UniProt ID generated a 3D protein structure. The segment concluded with cinematic 3D animations of molecule-protein binding and a wide shot of a large, purple-lit auditorium audience watching the presentation, as the speaker affirmed that BioNeMo NIMS are reinventing computational drug design, all accompanied by subtle, futuristic instrumental background music.

He emphasizes that it's "optimized depending on whether you have a single GPU, multi-GPU, or multi-node of GPUs." The diagram, which remains on screen, shows a layered stack with 'NVIDIA CUDA' and 'Kubernetes' at the base, supporting the core microservice with 'Industry Standard APIs,' 'Triton Inference Server,' 'Cloud Native Stack,' 'Enterprise Management,' 'TensorRT LLM and Triton,' 'Optimized Model,' and 'Customization Cache,' all built upon '100's of Millions of CUDA GPUs Installed Base.' Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black leather jacket and white sneakers, presented on a dark stage, holding a remote and gesturing expressively. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo were consistently visible. Huang introduced the 'Nemo retriever' and 'Nemo service' for creating 'digital humans,' exemplified by 'Diana,' an AI connected to 'Hippocratic AI's large language model for healthcare,' described as 'completely animated with AI.' He then detailed NVIDIA AI Foundry's collaborations with major companies. With SAP, which generates 87% of global commerce, they are building SAP Joule copilots using NVIDIA NeMo and DGX Cloud. For ServiceNow, which powers 85% of Fortune 500 people and customer service operations, they are building 'ServiceNow assist virtual assistants' using NVIDIA AI Foundry, DGX Cloud, NeMo, TensorRT, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise, a partnership dating back to 2018. Cohesity, backing up hundreds of exabytes of data for over 10,000 companies, is using NVIDIA AI Foundry to build their Gaia generative AI agent, integrating NVIDIA AI Enterprise, RAPIDS, cuVS, NeMo Retriever, and NIM Microservices. Snowflake, storing the world's digital warehouse in the cloud and serving over 3 billion queries daily for 10,000 enterprise customers, is building copilots with NVIDIA NeMo and NIMs, accelerating data and AI on NVIDIA. Finally, NetApp, storing nearly half the world's files on-prem, is leveraging NVIDIA AI Foundry to build chatbots and copilots, enabling NetApp customers to 'talk to their data' using NetApp Customer Data and NVIDIA NeMo Retriever for Gen AI.

Jensen continues, emphasizing the "great partnership with Dell" and stating that anyone building chatbots and generative AI will need an "AI factory," asserting that "nobody is better at building end-to-end systems of very large scale for the enterprise than Dell." The camera zooms in on him before panning out to reveal a large audience in a purple-lit auditorium, attentively watching and then clapping enthusiastically. Jensen acknowledges the audience's engagement, noting that "Michael is here, he's happy to take your order," and then introduces "Ladies and gentlemen, Michael Dell," prompting more applause. The scene returns to Jensen Huang, who gestures toward a new diagram illustrating a circular workflow connecting AI, Omniverse Digital Twin, and Robotic System, with corresponding hardware images (DGX, OVX, AGX). He begins to discuss "the next wave of robotics," or "physical AI," explaining that previous AI discussions focused on "one computer" where "data comes into one computer, lots of the world..." The stage remains dark and minimalist, with focused lighting on the speaker and the large screen, creating a high-contrast, professional atmosphere.

The video begins with a static technical diagram on a black background, illustrating a circular workflow connecting AI, Omniverse Digital Twin, and Robotic System, with corresponding hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX displayed in three vertical boxes on the right, highlighted in green and white. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang then appears on a minimally lit dark stage, wearing a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers. Holding a small black remote, he gestures expressively as he explains how AI imitates us by reading language to predict words and studying patterns, emphasizing that once it understands context, it's essentially imitating you. He details how data is fed into a system like DGX, compressed into a large language model with trillions of parameters, which then becomes the AI. Huang continues, stating that for the next wave of AI, where it understands the physical world, three computers will be needed. He elaborates on the first computer, an AI computer that will watch video and perform synthetic data generation, drawing parallels to human examples in text form. The date and logo remain on screen throughout his speech.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively with both hands as he explains how AI systems will "watch us, understand what is happening and try to adapt it for themselves into the context," emphasizing that because AI can generalize with foundation models, "maybe these robots can also perform in the physical world fairly generally." White text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are visible on screen. He continues, stating he just described "essentially what just happened in large language models, except the Chat GPT moment for robotics may be right around the corner." The scene then transitions to a wide shot, revealing a large green and white circular technical diagram behind him, illustrating the integration of AI, Omniverse Digital Twin, and Robotic System, with hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX displayed. Gesturing towards the diagram, Jensen proudly states, "we've been building the end-to-end systems for robotics for some time. I'm super, super proud of the work." He points out, "We have the AI system DGX," and then introduces "the lower system which is called AGX for autonomous systems, the world's first robotics processor." He elaborates on AGX, noting that when they first built it, people questioned its purpose, but it's a "SOC, it's one chip, it's designed to be very low power, but it's designed for high-speed sensor processing and AI." The camera cuts back to a close-up as he continues to speak with animated hand gestures, explaining that "if you want to run transformer..." maintaining the professional, tech-forward atmosphere against the dark background.

The video opens with a close-up of NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He speaks with a clear, confident voice, using dynamic hand gestures to emphasize his points as he explains that for "anything that moves," like a car running transformers, "we have the perfect computer for you. It's called the Jetson." The stage is dark, with a spotlight on him, creating a high-contrast, professional setting. On-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. He continues, detailing how "the DGX on top for training the AI, the Jetson is the autonomous processor, and in the middle, we need another computer." The camera occasionally zooms out to a wide shot, revealing Huang standing alone under the spotlight before a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple ambient lighting. He engages the audience, discussing how "large language models have the benefit of you providing your examples and then doing reinforcement learning human feedback." He then poses, "What is the reinforcement learning human feedback of a robot?" answering that it's "reinforcement learning physical feedback," which is how a robot is aligned and learns articulation and manipulation capabilities to adapt to the laws of physics. He concludes by stating the need for "a simulation engine that represents the world digitally for the robot so that the robot has a gym to go learn how to be a robot. We call that..."

The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, gesturing expressively on a dark stage, with the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo visible. Huang explains how the system will "redirect traffic" and provide "new waypoints" to robots and people, ensuring they "know exactly what to do." He then describes how a "warehouse" or "building" can be "talked to," even asking, "How are you feeling today?" as he details a system where Omniverse Cloud hosts virtual simulations and AI runs on DGX Cloud, all operating in real-time. As Jensen says, "Let's take a look," the visuals shift to an overhead 3D simulation of a vast warehouse, complete with pallets, forklifts, and workers, which then transitions to a real-world warehouse scene where workers in safety vests and hard hats operate forklifts and manage pallets. A dramatic, orchestral background music begins, and a deeper male voice-over explains that "The future of heavy industry starts as a digital twin," where "AI agents helping robots, workers, and infrastructure navigate unpredictable events in complex industrial spaces will be built and evaluated first in sophisticated digital twins," specifically mentioning "This Omniverse Digital Twin of a 100,000 sq..." before the audio fades. The dark, focused environment with stage lighting consistently highlights the speaker and the large screen, emphasizing the seamless connection between simulation and physical operations.

event_blocks.summary: The simulation environment, integrating digital workers, AMRs running Nvidia Isaac Perceptor, centralized activity maps from 100 simulated cameras using Nvidia Metropolis, and AMR route planning with Nvidia Co-op, is demonstrated. A multi-camera tracking interface displays live feeds and a top-down digital map tracking 30 individuals for software-in-loop testing of AI agents adapting to real-world unpredictability. An overhead simulation view overlays a dynamically adjusting network of nodes and paths, with red lines indicating conflicts, and a crash sound accompanies an incident where a shelf collapsed in Warehouse Aisle 3, blocking an AMR's planned route. An 'Agent Response' pop-up queries the unusual situation, and the system alerts to the collapsed shelf and fallen boxes, confirmed by Camera_048. Nvidia Metropolis updates an occupancy map to Co-op, calculating a new optimal route for the AMR to navigate around the obstruction, improving mission efficiency. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang explains that this system, powered by generative AI Metropolis Vision Foundation models, allows operators to ask questions in natural language. A technical diagram illustrates a digital twin system where real-world data feeds into NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud and DGX Cloud for applications like Metropolis MTMC and cuOpt Route Planning. Huang states that sensor data is created in simulation and passed to real-time AI running as NVIDIA Inference Microservices (NIMs), connecting to real sensors for continuous improvement of both the digital twin and AI models. He emphasizes that future facilities will be software-defined, tested within digital twins, with autonomous robotic stacks integrated through this system. Huang also announces Omniverse Cloud APIs, enabling applications to connect and provide digital twin capabilities, and reveals Omniverse has been integrated with the ability to 'chat USD' (universal scene description), allowing users to generate USD directly by speaking in English. The diagram shows 'Edify', 'ChatUSD', and 'DeepSearch' blocks processing prompts to output 3D USD, USD, and USD respectively, with DeepSearch connecting to a Data Lake, highlighting NVIDIA's cloud-based 3D content creation and AI integration ecosystem, enabling semantic information retrieval.

The segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote, gesturing expressively on a dark stage with focused lighting against a minimalist background. event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO (Jensen Huang), in a black leather jacket on a minimalist stage, highlights semantic encoding's application to 3D design, simulation, and generation, revealing the 'SIEMENS' and 'NVIDIA' logos on the screen behind him and announcing a partnership with Siemens, describing them as the 'world's largest industrial engineering and operations platform' connecting their 'crown jewel accelerator' to NVIDIA Omniverse. The scene transitions to a 3D-rendered industrial facility, a brightly lit warehouse where two warehouse workers, a woman with a tablet and a man with a clipboard, in yellow safety vests, white hard hats, and gloves, walk through an aisle as upbeat, futuristic music plays and a voiceover declares, 'Siemens technology is transformed everyday for everyone.' The video then showcases a Siemens Teamcenter X interface with a 3D model of a coffee machine, as a male voice introduces Teamcenter X as Siemens' leading product lifecycle management software, transitioning to a scene of a woman in a blue lab coat and a man in a white shirt and tie collaborating over a laptop displaying a green spherical conceptual design, integrating NVIDIA AI and Omniverse technologies into Teamcenter X. A diagram illustrates the integration of Siemens Xcelerator, RTX Render, and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud, emphasizing USD data flow via Omniverse APIs for data interoperability and physics-based rendering. The narrative shifts to a real-world shipyard with an 'HD Hyundai' ship under construction, transitioning into a detailed 3D simulation of the same ship within Siemens Teamcenter X, identifying HD Hyundai as a market leader in sustainable ship manufacturing. A diagram shows a user inputting a prompt ('atlantic ocean waves, remote, photography, ocean, sea, morning sky') into Siemens Xcelerator, connecting to RTX Render and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via Edify API, resulting in a photorealistic rendering of the ship in a maritime environment, with the voice concluding that Omniverse APIs allow Teamcenter X to unify and visualize massive engineering datasets interactively, integrating generative AI to create 3D objects or HDRI backgrounds, as a worker in a hard hat observes the digital twin of the ship's propeller and rudder.

event_blocks.summary: The presentation commenced with a detailed, grayscale 3D digital twin of a large ship's internal structure within Siemens Teamcenter X, showcasing intricate piping and machinery, as Roland Busch, CEO of Siemens, described it as an "ultra intuitive photoreal physics-based digital twin that eliminates waste and errors, delivering huge savings in cost and time." The view shifted to the ship's exterior, revealing 'HD HYUNDAI' branding, with Busch emphasizing its construction for collaboration across Siemens Xcelerator tools and teams. A technical diagram then illustrated the integration of Siemens Xcelerator with NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via USD APIs and Omniverse Channel APIs, while Busch highlighted the incorporation of "accelerated computing, generative AI, and Omniverse integration across the Siemens Xcelerator portfolio." Following applause, the scene transitioned to a real-world shipyard with a worker in a hard hat inspecting the hull, then to a simulation featuring animated workers and cranes. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, in a black leather jacket, entered the presentation stage, gesturing towards a large screen displaying the HD Hyundai shipyard digital twin, and humorously identified Busch as "a professional voice actor" and "a good friend of mine," drawing further applause from the audience bathed in purple lighting. The event concluded with a wide shot of Huang on stage before the expansive digital twin, underscoring the collaborative scale.

event_blocks.summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in his signature black leather jacket and attire, stood on a dimly lit stage on March 18, 2024, to unveil NVIDIA's integrated robotics and autonomous vehicle platforms. He detailed how the NVIDIA Robotics Platform, featuring Isaac Perceptor, Isaac Sim in Omniverse, and the Isaac Robotic Stack, empowers over 1.3 million robotics developers and 6,000 companies using the Orin computer. Huang emphasized the shift towards robots with perception capabilities, moving beyond pre-programmed AGVs to adaptive navigation using waypoints, and highlighted the 'Pose Grasp Manipulate' SDK for robotic arms, showcasing collaborations with Yaskawa, Universal Robots, PickNik, Solomon, Ready Robotics, and Franka. Transitioning to the automotive sector, he presented the DRIVE AV STACK, integrated within Omniverse via DRIVE SIM, and powered by MSFNET ROADNET PLANNET and DGX systems, with OVX and AGX facilitating the workflow. Huang announced that BYD, the world's largest EV company, is adopting the 'Drive Thor ASIL-D AV Computer & Stack,' a system designed for AI and capable of running safety-critical applications, which will be integrated into Mercedes and JLR vehicles, underscoring NVIDIA's commitment to building and opening up the entire robotic stack from hardware to software for widespread adoption.

event_blocks.summary: Initially, a technical diagram illustrating a robotics workflow with 'POSE GRASP MANIPULATE', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC MANIPULATOR STACK' connected to hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX was displayed against a black background, featuring a blue Yaskawa robotic arm on the left and a 360-degree camera view, robotic arm simulation, and Jetson Orin Robotic Computer & Stack on the right, alongside logos for Yaskawa, Universal Robots, PickNik, Solomon, Ready Robotics, and Franka, dated 'MARCH 18, 2024'. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, took the stage, explaining that computationally intensive processes are now 'CUDA accelerated' with the introduction of the 'world's first CUDA accelerated motion planner that is geometry aware,' highlighting its 'excellent perception for pose estimation of a 3D object' and ability to 'imagine what's around and how best to grap it.' As he spoke, the background shifted between 'PICKNIK' and 'SOLOMON' logos. Huang named this system 'Isaac Manipulator,' noting its available foundation pose, grip foundation, and articulation algorithms run on NVIDIA computers. The camera then pulled back to show him under a spotlight addressing a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple light. He transitioned to discussing the 'next generation of robotics,' predicting it 'will likely be a humanoid robotics,' which was accompanied by a new diagram for humanoid robotics featuring 'GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL', 'ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE', and 'GROOT STACK', connected to DGX, OVX, and AGX. This new diagram showed a black and teal exoskeleton humanoid robot on the left, a GROOT model diagram, a simulation of humanoid robots navigating terrain, and two Jetson Thor Robotic Computer & Stack units on the right. Subsequently, Jensen Huang's voice explained the 'necessary technology to imagine generalized human robotics,' stating that 'human robotics is likely easier' due to abundant 'imitation training data' because humans are constructed similarly. The scene shifted to Huang on a dark, minimally lit stage, holding a remote and gesturing, emphasizing that 'humanoid robotics will be much more useful in our world because we created the world' for human interaction, making them 'much more productive to deploy' in 'workstations, manufacturing, and logistics.' Jensen Huang names the system "Isaac Manipulator," noting that its foundation pose, grip foundation, and articulation algorithms are now available and run on NVIDIA computers. The camera pulls back to reveal him under a spotlight, addressing a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple light. He then transitions, stating, "We are starting to do some really great work in the next generation of robotics," which "will likely be a humanoid robotics." This statement coincides with a cut to a new diagram for humanoid robotics, replacing the previous one. This new diagram features a circular flow with 'GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL', 'ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE', and 'GROOT STACK', connected to DGX, OVX, and AGX.
